claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's get started. First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is your child?** Please tell me:
- If they're under 16 years old
- If they're between 16 and 19 years old
- If they're 20 years old or over

If you have multiple children, we can go through them one by one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have two children actually - Alex who's 16, and Blake who's 5 years old. Should we go through them one at a time?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Yes, absolutely! It's great that you have that information ready. We'll go through each child individually to assess eligibility for both Alex (16) and Blake (5).

Let's start with **Blake, who is 5 years old**, since that will be more straightforward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since Blake is only 5 years old, I assume Blake is not working. **Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?** 

(I'm assuming the answer is yes for a 5-year-old, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Blake doesn't work at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now, **is Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

In other words, Blake is not claiming benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in their own name, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - Blake doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now, **is Blake in any type of apprenticeship?**

(Again, for a 5-year-old this would be no, but I need to confirm for the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in an apprenticeship - they're only 5!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. **Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You'll qualify if **ANY** of these apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources (and not claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Which of these applies to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I normally live in the UK - I live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, **are you responsible for Blake?**

You're considered responsible if **ANY** of these apply:

1. **You live with Blake**
2. You contribute to Blake's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - including food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Which of these applies to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to ask: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?**

For example:
- Blake's other parent
- A grandparent
- Another family member or guardian

Please let me know:
- **No one else is claiming** for Blake
- **Someone else is claiming** but they've agreed to transfer it to you
- **Someone else is claiming** and there's a dispute about who should claim

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit for them, and Blake lives with them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - thank you for clarifying that. This is important information.

If Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake and Blake lives with them, then **you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake** because only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time.

However, if you and Blake's other parent wanted to **transfer the claim to you**, that would be possible if you both agree and Blake comes to live with you primarily. But based on what you've said, Blake lives with their other parent who is already claiming.

Let me ask a clarifying question: **Do you and Blake's other parent want to transfer the Child Benefit claim from them to you?** Or is Blake's other parent going to continue claiming, and you're just checking your eligibility for your other child, Alex?

Also, just to confirm: **Does Alex (your 16-year-old) live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we're not looking to transfer it - Blake's other parent will continue claiming for them. I'm really interested in whether I can claim for Alex. Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying! That makes sense. So Blake's other parent will continue claiming for Blake, and we need to focus on **Alex, who is 16 years old**.

You mentioned that **Alex doesn't live with you**. This is an important factor for responsibility.

Let me check: **Are you responsible for Alex in any of these ways?**

1. You contribute to Alex's upkeep by paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care - this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
2. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement that covers Alex's upkeep

Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you could still be eligible if you're contributing financially to their care. **Do either of these apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I help with Alex's expenses - I contribute towards things like clothes and school costs. I pay £50 a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful information! Let me check the current Child Benefit rates to see if your £50 per week contribution meets the requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! Your £50 per week contribution should be sufficient. The current Child Benefit rates are approximately £25 per week for the eldest/only child and around £16.50 for additional children, so your £50 per week contribution clearly exceeds the Child Benefit amount.

Now, since Alex is 16 years old, we need to go through some additional checks. Let me navigate to the appropriate path for 16-19 year olds.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now for Alex, who is 16, I need to ask:

**Is Alex currently in education or training, OR has Alex left education recently (within the last 20 weeks)?**

Please tell me which applies:
1. **Alex is currently in education or training**
2. **Alex left education recently** (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** - Alex is not in education/training and didn't leave recently

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in education - studying for A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! A-levels are an approved qualification for Child Benefit. Now I need to check a few more things about Alex's situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! A-levels are definitely approved. Now I need to confirm a few details about Alex's education:

1. **Is Alex studying at least 12 hours per week of supervised study?** (This is typically the case for full-time A-level students)

2. **Is this education funded by an employer or part of a training agreement for a future job with an employer?**

3. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?** (If yes, we need to check if Alex is financially dependent on you)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is studying full-time, so definitely more than 12 hours a week. No, it's not funded by an employer - just regular sixth form college. And no, Alex isn't married.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's all great news. Now let me check a couple more things about Alex's situation:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

It's okay if Alex has a part-time job, as long as it's less than 24 hours per week.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - just focuses on school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now, **is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in their own right**, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

In other words, Alex is not claiming benefits in their own name, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! **Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

You mentioned Alex is doing A-levels at sixth form college, so I assume not, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship - just A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! We've already established that you normally live in the UK (in Manchester), so that requirement is met. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good! We've already established that you are responsible for Alex through your £50 per week contribution towards clothes and school costs, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, this is an important question: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example, is Alex's other parent (who Alex lives with) claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's living situation. **Which of these applies to Alex?**

1. **In hospital** - Alex is currently in hospital
2. **In care** - Alex is in local authority care
3. **Fostered** - Alex is in foster care
4. **Normal living** - Alex is living normally (not in hospital, care, or foster care)

Based on what you've told me (Alex is at sixth form college studying A-levels), I assume it's normal living, but please confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is living normally - not in hospital, care, or fostering.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final question: **Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the local council is not covering Alex's living costs or care, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

### Summary of Your Eligibility:

**For Blake (5 years old):** ❌ **Not Eligible**
- Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for them
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child at a time
- If you wanted to claim in the future, you would need to arrange a transfer with Blake's other parent

**For Alex (16 years old):** ✅ **ELIGIBLE**
- Alex meets all the requirements:
  - In approved education (A-levels at sixth form college)
  - Studying full-time (more than 12 hours per week)
  - Not working or working less than 24 hours per week
  - Not receiving benefits in their own right
  - Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- You meet all the claimant requirements:
  - You normally live in the UK (Manchester)
  - You are responsible for Alex through your £50/week financial contributions
  - No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
  - Alex is living normally and the council isn't paying for them

### Next Steps to Claim for Alex:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** if you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year
5. **Payments are made every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can start your claim here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about the application process or your eligibility?

reasonings: Blake is under 16 years old and meets all the child eligibility criteria (not working full-time, not receiving benefits in own right, not in an apprenticeship). You meet the residency requirements (normally live in the UK) and responsibility requirements (live with Blake). However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time. Since there is no agreed transfer of the claim, you are not eligible to claim for Blake.

Alex is 16 years old and in approved non-advanced education (A-levels) studying full-time (more than 12 hours per week of supervised study). Alex is not working (less than 24 hours per week), not receiving benefits in own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. You meet the UK residency requirements (normally live in Manchester). You are responsible for Alex through your financial contributions of £50 per week towards clothes and school costs, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25 per week for eldest child). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is living normally (not in hospital, care, or fostering) and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. You meet all eligibility requirements for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have two children: Blake (5 years old) and Alex (16 years old). For Blake, you cannot claim Child Benefit because Blake's other parent is already claiming for them, and only one person can claim per child. For Alex, you ARE eligible to claim Child Benefit because you meet all requirements: Alex is in approved education (A-levels), you contribute financially to Alex's upkeep (£50/week), you live in the UK, no one else is claiming for Alex, and all other criteria are satisfied.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

child_evaluations.reasoning: Blake is under 16 years old and meets all the child eligibility criteria (not working full-time, not receiving benefits in own right, not in an apprenticeship). You meet the residency requirements (normally live in the UK) and responsibility requirements (live with Blake). However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time. Since there is no agreed transfer of the claim, you are not eligible to claim for Blake.

Alex is 16 years old and in approved non-advanced education (A-levels) studying full-time (more than 12 hours per week of supervised study). Alex is not working (less than 24 hours per week), not receiving benefits in own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. You meet the UK residency requirements (normally live in Manchester). You are responsible for Alex through your financial contributions of £50 per week towards clothes and school costs, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25 per week for eldest child). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is living normally (not in hospital, care, or fostering) and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. You meet all eligibility requirements for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: You have two children: Blake (5 years old) and Alex (16 years old). For Blake, you cannot claim Child Benefit because Blake's other parent is already claiming for them, and only one person can claim per child. For Alex, you ARE eligible to claim Child Benefit because you meet all requirements: Alex is in approved education (A-levels), you contribute financially to Alex's upkeep (£50/week), you live in the UK, no one else is claiming for Alex, and all other criteria are satisfied.
